## Comparing Floating Point Numbers with `==` in IEEE 754

The short answer is: **it depends on the situation**.

---

## When `==` is UNSAFE

### Computed/calculated values
```python
# Rounding errors make this unpredictable
0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3        # False! (0.1 + 0.2 = 0.30000000000000004)
sqrt(2) * sqrt(2) == 2  # False!
sin(pi) == 0            # False! (result is ~1.2e-16)
```

### Iterative/accumulated operations
```c
float sum = 0.0f;
for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) sum += 0.1f;
sum == 1.0f;  // False - errors accumulate
```

---

## When `==` IS Safe

### Exact integer values (within precision limits)
```python
1.0 == 1.0    # True  - exact representation
2.0 + 2.0 == 4.0  # True  - exact
```

### Assignments / no computation
```c
float x = 3.14f;
float y = x;
x == y;  // Always true - same bits
```

### Powers of 2 and certain fractions
```python
0.5 + 0.25 == 0.75  # True - exact in binary
```

### Special values (by IEEE 754 spec)
```python
float('inf') == float('inf')   # True
float('-inf') == float('-inf') # True
float('nan') == float('nan')   # FALSE! NaN != NaN always
```

---

## The NaN Special Case
```c
double x = NaN;
x == x;   // FALSE - NaN is never equal to anything, including itself
x != x;   // TRUE  - the standard way to check for NaN
isnan(x); // TRUE  - better/clearer way to check
```

---
